name: Friction
subject: physics
summary: Friction opposes motion. It depends on surface roughness and how hard surfaces press together — friend and foe.

crushed.title: Friction = opposing force
crushed.text: Always acts opposite to the direction of motion (or attempted motion).

crushed.title: Depends on two things
crushed.text: Surface roughness + normal force (how hard surfaces press). NOT on area of contact.

crushed.title: Friend: walking, writing, gripping
crushed.text: Without friction you couldn't walk — feet would slip. Brakes stop cars.

crushed.title: Foe: wear, heat, energy loss
crushed.text: Machines lose energy to friction. Parts wear out. Engines overheat.

crushed.title: Reduce friction
crushed.text: Lubricants (oil, grease), ball bearings, polishing surfaces, streamlining.

items.label: Static friction
items.detail: Object at rest — must overcome to start moving. Highest value

items.label: Sliding friction
items.detail: Object already moving — opposes ongoing motion

items.label: Rolling friction
items.detail: Wheel rolling on surface — much less than sliding

items.label: Fluid friction (drag)
items.detail: Object moving through air or water — depends on speed & shape

left.label: Static Friction
points: Max value just before motion starts

right.label: Sliding Friction
points: Why pushing a box gets easier once it moves

flows.title: How Friction Lets You Walk
flows.description: You push backward on the ground — friction pushes you forward.

steps.label: Leg muscles
steps.description: Your foot tries to slide backward on the ground.

steps.label: Friction
steps.description: Static friction opposes backward slide — equal and opposite forward force.

steps.label: Body moves
steps.description: Forward friction force propels your body ahead.

steps.label: Ice?
steps.description: On ice, friction is tiny — foot slides, you fall.

flows.title: How Brakes Stop a Car
flows.description: Brake pads create friction against wheels to convert kinetic energy to heat.

steps.label: Brake pads
steps.description: Pads press hard against rotating wheel disc.

steps.label: Friction
steps.description: Sliding friction between pad and disc slows the wheel.

steps.label: Heat
steps.description: Kinetic energy converted to heat — discs can glow red.

steps.label: Stop
steps.description: When friction force balances motion, car stops.

keyTerms.definition: Force opposing relative motion between surfaces in contact
keyTerms.analogy: A grumpy gatekeeper that fights any movement

keyTerms.definition: Substance (oil, grease) placed between surfaces to reduce friction
keyTerms.analogy: Slippery middleman between two fighting surfaces

keyTerms.definition: Shaping objects to reduce fluid friction (drag)
keyTerms.analogy: Why fish and rockets are pointy at the front

keyTerms.definition: Force pressing surfaces together — perpendicular to surface
keyTerms.analogy: How hard you're squishing two surfaces together

demos.title: Walking — Push Back, Friction Forward
demos.description: You push ground backward; friction pushes you forward.

steps.label: Foot pushes ground
steps.description: Muscles apply backward force on the ground.

steps.label: Friction reacts
steps.description: Ground friction pushes foot forward — you move ahead.

steps.label: Braking
steps.description: Brake pads use friction to convert motion into heat and stop.

chapterSummary.overview: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces in contact. This chapter covers types of friction (static, sliding, rolling), factors affecting it, advantages (walking, writing, gripping) and disadvantages (wear, heat loss), and methods to increase or reduce friction.
keyPoints: Static friction prevents motion; sliding friction acts during motion; rolling friction is smallest.
Friction depends on nature of surfaces and how hard they are pressed together.
Useful: walking, brakes, holding objects; Harmful: wear, energy wasted as heat.
Reduce friction: lubricants, ball bearings, polishing, streamlining.
Increase friction: treaded tyres, brake pads, kabaddi players rubbing hands with soil.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Give examples where friction is useful vs harmful. Explain how lubricants work. Compare static, sliding, rolling friction.
